courses.name: GENERAL CHEMISTRY
courses.purpose: To teach the basic concepts and principles of chemistry. To provide the theoretical and practical knowledge together. To improve the ability of problem solving skill and to make critical decisions. To give the importance of chemistry on the daily life. To help the students thinking positively, logical and to understand the principles of nature.
courses.description: The scope of chemistry and stoichiometry, atoms and the atomic theories, the periodic table and some atomic properties, chemical bonding, molecular geometry, gases and gas laws, liquids, solids, solutions and their physical properties, thermochemistry, principles of chemical equilibrium, acids and bases, thermodynamic.
learning_outcomes: Student, who passed the course satisfactorily will be able to identify and apply atomic theories and useful relationships from the periodic table,
Make calculations with using stochiometry in chemical reactions,
Solve different problems about liquid solutions and gases.
Make applications about heat, work, enthalpy and internal energy.
Set up the three dimensional shape of molecular compounds with using their chemical bonding knowledge and some other bond theories.
Show the crystal structures of solids and skills to solve related problems
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Properties of matter, classification of matter, matter measurement (SI units), significant figuresThe law of conservation of masses, law constant proportions, Dalton’s theory, the discovery of electrons, atomic nucleus, atomic masses, Avogadro’s number and mole concept
Hafta 2: Chemical compounds and formulas, equations, balancing chemical oxidation steps, redox reactions, the limiting component and the yield of the reaction.
Hafta 3: Properties of gases, gas pressure,the simple gas laws, the ideal gas equation, chemical reactions, gases, gas mixtures, kinetic theory of gases, real gases and van der Waals equation
Hafta 4: Some of the terms of thermochemistry heat,heats of reaction and calorimetry, work, the first law of thermodynamic, the internal energy, entalphy, Hess’s law, the standart formation heats
Hafta 5: Electromagnetic radiation, atomic spectra,quantum theory, Bohr’s atomic model, the new uantum mechanics, quantum numbers, electron distributions
Hafta 6: periodic table of the elements, classification of the elements, the relationship between the distributions of the electron with the periodic table of elements, atomic radii,

Hafta 7: Introduction to Lewis theory, and teh classification of bonds, covalent bonding, multiple covalent bonds, polar covalent bonds, electronegativity, Lewis structureIntroduction to Lewis theory, and teh classification of bonds, covalent bonding, multiple covalent bonds, polar covalent bonds, electronegativity, Lewis structure,
Hafta 8: Lewis structures, formal charge, the octet rule deviations, resonance, molecular forms, VSEPR theory, the sample molecules

Hafta 10: Intermolecular forces and some propertiesof liquids, viscosity, surface tension, vapor pressure, Clasius-Clapeyron equation, the critical point of the phase diagrams(water), van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds.
Hafta 11: Solutions, solution concentrations, dissolution and dissolution enthalpy, heat is soluble in solutions of ionic calculation, the effect of temperature and pressure on solubility

Hafta 12: Vapor pressure of solutions, osmotic pressure, freezing point depression, boiling point elevation, electrolyte solutions,Dynamic equilibrium, the equilibrium constant, Le-Chatalier principle, factors affecting equilibrium
Hafta 13: Arrhenius theory, acid-base theory, pH concept, strong acids and bases, weak acids and bases, polyprotic acids, acid-base feature, showing the ions, acid-base equilibrium calculations

Hafta 14: Spontaneity, disorder entropy, , the second law of thermodynamics ,free-energy change, after the process between free energy and equilibrium, net, determine the direction of the reaction, the factors affecting equilibrium

Hafta 15: Spontaneity, disorder entropy, , the second law of thermodynamics ,free-energy change, after the process between free energy and equilibrium, net, determine the direction of the reaction, the factors affecting equilibrium

courses.name: ACADEMIC ENGLISH I
courses.purpose: The purpose of this class is to make students gain competence in the four language skills (reading, listening, speaking, writing) and to help them follow their classes in English more easily. It also aims to consolidate their preparatory-school knowledge, support their progress to upper levels, and improve critical thinking and writing through portfolio projects and online exercises.
courses.description: This course focuses on developing students' English language skills through reading, listening, speaking, and writing activities. It includes academic reading and writing tasks, essay writing, vocabulary development, discussion and debate practice, listening comprehension, and online exercises using course materials such as The Compass 1 and academic writing resources.
learning_outcomes: Improve reading skills
Improve writing skills
Improve listening skills
Improve speaking skills
Develop analytical thinking related to their field
Improve advanced vocabulary knowledge
Analyze reading passages on current and different topics
Practice exercises online using technology
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course; Introduction to The Compass, Route to Academic English 1; Views On “Change”; finding the relationship between ideas; writing a quote on the concept of change
Hafta 2: Views On “Change”; listening for specific information and the main idea; expressing opinions; understanding long sentences
Hafta 3: Unit 3: The Essay; the essay process
Hafta 4: Unit 3: The Essay; writing the essay; characteristics of formal writing; the introduction
Hafta 5: Unit 3: The Essay; the body paragraphs; the conclusion; the title; Unit 1: Changing The Self; listening and speaking activities on plastic surgery
Hafta 6: Unit 1: Changing The Self; reading on transhumanism; previewing, skimming, scanning, identifying points of reference
Hafta 7: Unit 1: Changing The Self; listening on transhumanism and the authentic self; speaking debate on smart drugs; expressing an opinion and practicing debate language
Hafta 9: Unit 2: Changing Communications; reading on online identities; guessing vocabulary and dealing with long sentences; writing a cause and effect essay

Hafta 10: Unit 2: Changing Communications; focusing on a skill; guessing vocabulary, cognates, contextual clues, world knowledge, and parts of the word; writing a cause and effect essay; organization and distinguishing between cause and effect

Hafta 11: Unit 2: Changing Communications; listening on social media and changes in communication; writing a cause and effect essay; sentence structure preparation for writing; quoting materials from outside sources; portfolio assignment
Hafta 12: Unit 2: Changing Communications; reading on social media and the public sphere; guessing vocabulary and collocations
Hafta 13: Unit 2: Changing Communications; strengthening the use of dictionaries; listening and speaking on a TED Talk by Allison Graham about how social media makes us unsocial
Hafta 14: Unit 2: Changing Communications; speaking on fake identities in social media; practicing debate and discussion; revision for the final exam
resources: David Hill, Pearson, Longman
Cause & Effect Essay (Longman Academic Writing Series-5, pages 64-84)
Gülen, G., Hasanbaşoğlu, B., Şeşen, E., & Tokdemir, G. (2014). Compass: The Route to Academic English I. Ankara: Nüans Publishing

courses.name: CALCULUS I
courses.purpose: To provide the concepts of functions, limits, continuity, differentiation and integration. To provide the knowledge of applications of differentiation and integration. To give an ability to apply knowledge of mathematics on engineering problems.
courses.description: Functions of a Single Variable, Limits and Continuity, Derivatives, Applications of Derivatives, Sketching Graphs of Functions, Asymptotes, Integration, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Applications of Integrals, Polar Coordinates, Transcendental Functions, Techniques of Integration, Indeterminate Forms, L'Hopital's Rule.
learning_outcomes: Compute the limit of various functions, use the concepts of the continuity, use the rules of differentiation to differentiate functions
Sketch the graph of a function using asymptotes, critical points and the derivative test for increasing/decreasing and concavity properties.
Set up max/min problems and use differentiation to solve them
Evaluate integrals by using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
Learns transcendental functions and evaluate integrals using techniques of integration
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Functions of a Single Variable
Hafta 2: Functions of a Single Variable, Limits of functions, Squeeze Theorem
Hafta 3: Limits at infinity, Asymptotes, Continuity
Hafta 4: Derivative, tangent and normal lines, differentiation rules, chain rule,higher order derivatives
Hafta 5: Implicit differentiation, Mean Value Theorem, Increasing and decreasing functions, derivatives of trigonometric functions
Hafta 7: Related rates, indeterminate forms, L'Hopital's Rules, Extreme Values, Critical Points
Hafta 9: The first derivative test, finding extreme values, concavity, the second derivative test
Hafta 10: Sketching graph of a function, Optimization problems
Hafta 11: Integration, areas as limits of sums, definite integrals, properties of definite integrals
Hafta 12: The fundamentral theorem of calculus, the method of substitiution, area of plane regions
Hafta 13: Integration techniques: Substitution rule, Integration by parts
Hafta 14: Improper integrals of type I, Improper integrals of type 2

R.A. Adams, Calculus: A complete course 8-th revised ed. , Prentice Hall, 2013.

courses.name: PHYSICS I
courses.purpose: The course objective is to enable students to write equations of motion for simple mechanical systems and integrate these equations. When direct integration is not feasible, students are expected to make predictions based on conservation laws. The course also aims to help students apply Newton’s laws and conservation laws to dynamical systems.
courses.description: Units, Physical Quantities, and Vectors. Motion Along a Straight Line. Motion in Two or Three Dimensions. Newton's Laws of Motion. Applying Newton's Laws. Work and Kinetic Energy. Potential Energy and Energy Conservation. Momentum, Impulse, and Collisions. Rotation of Rigid Bodies. Dynamics of Rotational Motion. Equilibrium and Elasticity.
learning_outcomes: Basic operation with vectors (addition, scalar and vectorial product)
Kinematics in one and two dimensions
Application of Newton's laws to fundamental problems of mechanics
Concept of conservative force
Mechanics of rigid bodies rotating about a fixed axis and gyroscope motion
Using conservation laws when direct integration of motion is not feasible
Basic applications of Newton universal gravitation law
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Physics and Measurement and Vectors
Hafta 2: Motion in One Dimension and Motion in Two Dimensions
Hafta 3: The Laws of Motion and Other Applications of Newton’s Laws
Hafta 5: Potential Energy and energy conversation
Hafta 7: Rotation of a Rigid Object About a Fixed Axis
Hafta 8: Angular Momentum, Static Equilibrium and Elasticity
Hafta 13: work and heat in thermodynamic processes
Hafta 14: Heat Engines, Entropy, and the Second Law of Thermodynamics
resources: Fundamentals of Physics, H. RESNICK, Vol I, John Wiley & Sons, 1981.
Physics for Scientist & Engineers with Modern Physics, R. A. SERWAY, Vol. I, Sounders Collage Publishing, 1992.

courses.name: PHYSICS LABORATORY I
courses.purpose: To apply basic physics concepts through laboratory experiments and to improve understanding of natural phenomena and scientific events.
courses.description: This course covers general information about experiments, the use and measurement of test instruments, analysis of experimental data, statistics, error calculation and uncertainties, and a series of laboratory topics including measurement, moment, modulus of elasticity, Hooke’s law, Newton’s 2nd law, moment of inertia and angular acceleration, projectile motion, and conservation of mechanical energy.
learning_outcomes: Gain the knowledge and skills necessary to use experimental methods and data analysis techniques.
Students have the ability to participate actively in group works.
Students gain the ability to take responsibility and have a principle.
Students develop the ability to make oral and written presentations.
Can work independently and jointly with stakeholders on physics-related issues and use the ability of abstract-analytical thinking.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: General information about experiments, introducing and measuring test instruments.
Hafta 2: Analysis of experiment data, statistics, error calculation and uncertainties
Hafta 10: Moment of inertia and angular acceleration
resources: Laboratory sheets
Physics I For Scientist And Engineers - Serway

courses.purpose: To introduce the subjects, working areas, terminology and concepts of Software Engineering; to inform about the working principles of computers, binary numbers, base transformations, software types, and hardware techniques. To arouse consciousness about algorithms and data types. To learn how computer handles floating point numbers in some standards. To write simple Python programming application, input output handling, and functions.

courses.name: COMPUTER PROGRAMMING I
courses.purpose: An introduction to programming from both design and programming standpoints. Syntax and semantics of programming languages. Programming style. Program debugging and testing. Data representation. Simple arithmetic expressions, decision and control statements. Arrays. Introduction to standard libraries. Structured programming technique will be introduced along with the usage of C language.
courses.description: This course will introduce the basic elements of a structural and imperative programming language. The C language will be used. Topics include the concept of type, main types, expressions, standard functions, input/output statements, type conversion, flow of control structures, ifthenelse, loop structures, whiledo, repeat until, fortodo, case statements; procedures and functions, modularity in programming, global and local variables, pointers, dynamic variables, and arrays.
learning_outcomes: Explain computer based problem-solving and algorithm.
Define the basic concepts of structured programming
Express arithmetic and logical functions in C programming language.
Define the concepts of memory management and pointer

courses.name: TURKISH LANGUAGE I
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to enable students to gain the ability to use the language correctly and effectively by comprehending the structure, meaning and functioning features of Turkish.
courses.description: Within the scope of the course, language; its function in the relations between thought, culture and society is discussed, the historical development of the Turkish language and its place among the world languages are introduced. It includes students' use of Turkish in oral and written expression in a correct, clear, consistent and aesthetic way, developing language awareness and realizing the value of language as a carrier of cultural identity.
learning_outcomes: Learning the structural and functional features of Turkish
Comprehending the definition of language, its place in human life, and the language-culture-nation connection
Learning the basic grammar rules of Turkish such as phonetics, vocabulary, sentence structure, and paragraph structure
Developing the ability to use the language correctly, effectively, and beautifully in written and oral expression
Being able to express oneself correctly in social life and comment with a critical perspective
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Classification of languages ??and the place of Turkish in language families
Hafta 3: Language contacts and language policies

courses.name: HISTORY OF THE TURKISH REVOLUTION I
courses.purpose: All phases of the national struggle, conventions, treaties and battles are examined from 1914 until October 29, 1923.
courses.description: Revolution and similar concepts; the collapse of the Ottoman Empire; the entry of the Ottoman Empire into the First World War and the Dardanelles Campaign; the preparation phase of the War of Independence and Atatürk’s arrival in Samsun; conventions and contributions to the National Struggle; the opening of Parliament and its works; internal revolts and edicts against it; the establishment of the regular army; and the wars in the War of Independence and their importance in history.
learning_outcomes: Understanding the meaning and importance of the Turkish War of Independence
Information and the ability to comment about the Turkish War of Independence and the preparatory conferences
The importance of the Congress to inform the Turkish people on the war is investigated.
Internal revolts and the applications of the concept of Kuvay-ı Millîye in the War of Independence are investigated.
The opening of Parliament and the process of transition to democracy, including the proclamation of the Republic, are examined.
The battles of the Turkish War of Independence are examined and discussed in the context of Military History and their political consequences.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: The concept of revolution, Ottoman Empire’s entrance to First World War, Gallipoli Campaign
Hafta 2: Atatürk’s coming to the Samsun and works, Erzurum and congress of Western Anatolia, contributions of Kuvay-ı Milliye to the Turkish War of Independence
Hafta 3: Kuvay-ı Milliye to the Turkish War of Independence
Hafta 4: Sivas Congress, the coming of Atatürk and Heyet-i Temsiliye to Ankara
Hafta 5: The gathering of the last Meclis-i Mebusan in Istanbul and acceptance of the Misak-ı Milliye
Hafta 6: The opening of Parliament, the nature and work of the Assembly
Hafta 7: Law of Hıyanat-i Vataniye, the establishment and operations of the Independence Tribunals
Hafta 9: Eastern Operations, war with Armenian gangs and the Treaty of Gyumri
Hafta 10: Eastern Operations, war with Armenian gangs and the Treaty of Gyumri
Hafta 11: First and Second İnönü Wars, the analyzing of Kütahya and Eskişehir in terms of military and political aspects, the London Conference
Hafta 12: Battle of Sakarya, Kars and Ankara Treaties
Hafta 13: Büyük Taarruz, Mudanya Armistice Agreement, Lozan Peace Treaty and its importance

courses.name: APPLIED LINEAR ALGEBRA
courses.purpose: To gain the ability to apply the concepts of linear algebra to engineering problems.
courses.description: Matrices, vectors, linear systems of equations, Gauss-Jordan elimination, echelon forms, rank, determinants, vector spaces, linear dependence/independence, basis, span, orthogonal projection, linear transformations, eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
learning_outcomes: Solve linear systems of equations more efficiently.
Convert matrices into desired form by applying elementary operations.
Compute and interpret on basis, dimension and linear independence of vectors.
Determine four subspaces of a matrix.
Determine eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix.
resources: Linear Algebra for Engineers and Scientists using Matlab, K. Hardy, Pearson Education, 2005.
Poole - Linear Algebra A Modern Introduction 4th c2015 txtbk, Elementary Linear Algebra, Larson and Falvo, Cengage Learning, 2009.

courses.name: PHYSICS II
courses.purpose: To relate the electromagnetic field to its sources within the frame of electrostatics, to introduce Maxwell's equations in vacuum, and to provide an introduction to electromagnetic waves.
courses.description: Coulomb laws and electrical field, Gauss law, electrical potential, capacitance, electrostatic energy and properties of insulators, current and resistance, DC circuits, the magnetic field, sources of magnetic field, Faraday's law, inductance, magnetic field in matter, electromagnetic oscillations and AC circuits, Maxwell equations, and electromagnetic waves.
learning_outcomes: To relate static electrical field to a continuous or discreet charge distribution (Coulomb law)
Computation of electrical field using symmetries of charge distribution
Energy of static electrical field. Electrical potential
Electrical current and application of Ohm law
The effect of magnetic field on moving charge and to relate static magnetic field to its sources
Maxwell's laws in the vacuum and alternative current
Electromagnetic waves
weekly_topics: Hafta 8: The effects of magnetic field
Hafta 10: The production of magnetic field
Hafta 12: Inductance and oscillations in LC circuit
resources: YOUNG,H.D.,FREEDMAN,RA, 2008, SEAR’S AND ZEMANSKY UNIVERSITY PHYSICS, Pearson Addison Wesley, ISBN:0-321-50130-.
Getty,W.E.,Kelller,M.J,Stove, 1993, Fizik II, Literatur yayıncılık, ISBN:975-7860-530.

courses.name: PHYSICS LABORATORY II
courses.purpose: Reinforce Physics 2 (Electrical) course information. Learning by applying the logical steps of the experimental study: conceptualization, planning, implementation, data collection, processing, scrutiny, presentation. Improving the ability to work in the group. To learn and apply the principles of working, usage. Understanding and applying the concepts of sensitivity, error, uncertainty in experimental measurements.
courses.description: General information about experiments, introducing and measuring test instruments, analysis of experiment data, statistics, error calculation and uncertainties, analysis of an experiment.
learning_outcomes: Gain the knowledge and skills necessary to use experimental methods and data analysis techniques.
Students have the ability to participate actively in group works
Students gain the ability to take responsibility and have a principle
Students develop the ability to make oral and written presentations.
Can work independently and jointly with stakeholders on physics-related issues and use the ability of abstract-analytical thinking.

Hafta 4: Charging curve of a capacitor

courses.name: COMPUTER PROGRAMMING II
courses.purpose: This course introduces the students to the fundamental concepts of structured programming.
courses.description: This course will introduce the basic elements of a structural and imperative programming language. The C language will be used. Topics include the concept of type, main types, expressions, standard functions, input/output statements, type conversion, flow of control structures, if then else, loop structures, while do, repeat until, for to do, case statements; procedures and functions, modularity in programming, global and local variables, pointers, dynamic variables, arrays, structures, unions, bit manipulations, enumerations, and file processing.
learning_outcomes: Use different application areas such as the properties of the C programming language.
Develop algorithm design and program writing
Define concepts in the design of visual programming languages
Develop new programming languages by using knowledge and experience.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course content, Objectives, Grading, Exams
Hafta 2: Introduction to C programming: Variables and assignments, input, and output
Hafta 3: Structured Program Development: Control structures
Hafta 4: Structured Program Development: Nested control statements, Increment and Decrement Operators
Hafta 6: Program Control: Loops, Multiple-Selection Statement
Hafta 7: Functions that return a value Parameter Passing Formal and Actual Parameters Scope of Variables

courses.name: TURKISH LANGUAGE II
courses.purpose: Turkish Language has been designated a compulsory course by the Council of Higher Education (YÖK). The general objectives of the course are to help students understand the structure and functioning of the Turkish language, develop their ability to use the language in written and spoken expression, raise awareness of the native language, teach the historical development and linguistic characteristics of Turkish, and improve listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills.
courses.description: The course content focuses on the fundamental structural and functional characteristics of language. It covers the nature and environment of language, language-culture-communication, the classification of languages, the place of Turkish among world languages, language contacts, and language policies. It also examines the structural characteristics of Turkish, including phonetics, vocabulary, and syntax, paragraph knowledge, and the rules for accurate and effective written expression such as spelling, punctuation, and grammatical errors.
learning_outcomes: Comprehend factors that ruin the formation of speech and speech
Comprehend the importance of effective speech
Practice methods and techniques of effective speech
Comprehend the difference between verbal expression and written expression
Distinguish the types of prepared and unprepared speeches
Use the body language efficiently in communication
Use the methods and techniques for preparing a planned speech
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: SPEAKING AND LISTENING: What is speech? Why is Speaking Important? Developing Speaking Skills
Hafta 2: SPEAKING AND LISTENING: Assessment of Speaking Skills, Points to Consider for Proper Pronunciation

Hafta 12: TYPES OF SPEECH: Features and Types of Prepared and Unprepared Speeches : Jon Interview, Discussion, Opening Speeches, Introduction Speeches, Panel, Open Session

courses.name: HISTORY OF THE TURKISH REVOLUTION II
courses.purpose: The stages of the Republican history are examined from October 29, 1923 until the day.
courses.description: Republican Period Ataturk Revolutions, Atatürk Principles, Atatürk Period Foreign Policy, Second World War and Turkey, After World War II Turkey.

analyze the different roles of these actors and facts
coment the concepts such as independence, liberty/freedom and national sovereignty
evaluate the process of the foundation of modern Turkey with her democratic, seculare and social institutions as well as the state of justice
analyze/evaluate the extending globalization that started in the end of the 20. Century
compare and describe the outcomes of the ‘new age’

courses.name: PRINCIPLES OF OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH AND SAFETY I
courses.purpose: The course aims to present the importance of occupational health and safety for workers, employers, and employees, and to provide a structure that combines theory and practice.
courses.description: Objectives and content of the course, outlines of the course, the aim of health and safety, basic concepts of health and safety such as injuries at work, occupational disease, historical development of health and safety, health and safety applications in Turkey and all over the world, health and safety legislations, occupational health and safety standard: OHSAS 1800, organization of health and safety, duties and responsibilities of those who are responsible for health and safety, responsibilities of government, employers and workers, types of incident and accident, occupational disease, prevention methods of diseases and accidents.
learning_outcomes: Students will gain knowledge on the basic concepts related to occupational safety and health of workers.
Students will be able to analyze effectiveness and efficiency of occupational safety in administrative processes.
Students will be able to adopt risk, prevention, and safety culture.
Students will be able to understand Engineer's responsibility the terms of occupational safety.

courses.name: DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
courses.purpose: This course provides an introduction to the basic theoretical concepts and applications of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODE). It aims to introduce students to real-life systems modeled by means of differential equations and their appropriate solution techniques.
courses.description: First Order Differential Equations, Second Order Linear Equations, Higher Order Linear Equations, the Laplace Transform, and Systems of First Order Differential Equations.
learning_outcomes: identify ODEs and their qualitative properties
appreciate the importance of establishing the existence and uniqueness of solutions
solve analytically a wide range of first order ODEs as well as Initial Value Problems (IVPs)
solve analytically a wide range of higher order linear homogeneous /nonhomogeneous ODEs with constant coefficients
solve higher order nonlinear differential equations
use Laplace transform for the solution of a wide range of ODEs and the systems formed by ODEs
solve the linear ODEs/system of ODEs with various right side functions including zero, constants, exponentials, sinusoids, step functions, impulses, and superpositions of these functions

Hafta 2: Separable Equations and Applications Linear First-Order Equations
Hafta 3: Substitution Methods and Exact Equations Homogeneous Equations Bernoulli Equations
Hafta 4: Second-Order Linear Equations: An Introduction Wronskians of Solutions
Hafta 5: Linear Second-Order Equations with Constant Coefficients Nonhomogeneous Equations and Undetermined Coefficients
Hafta 6: Cauchy-Euler Equations Variation of Parameters
Hafta 8: The Laplace Transform General Properties of Transforms
Hafta 10: The Inverse Laplace Transforms Solution of Initial Value Problems using Laplace Transform
Hafta 11: Periodic and Piecewise Continuous Input Functions Transforms of Periodic Functions
Hafta 12: Introduction to Systems of Differential Equations The Method of Elimination
Hafta 13: The Eigenvalue Method for Homogeneous Systems
Hafta 14: Matrix Exponentials and Linear Systems Fundamental Matrix Solutions Matrix Exponential Solutions

courses.name: OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
courses.purpose: Students will have a chance to learn basic concepts of Object Oriented Programming (OOP). Students will also learn to design a solution for problems using OOP in Java.
courses.description: This course covers the basic concepts and principles of object oriented programming using Java. Topics include Java and NetBeans environments, variables, selections, loops, arrays, methods, objects and classes, inheritance and polymorphism, exception handling, encapsulation, abstract classes and interfaces, generics, strings and texts, and graphical user interface (GUI) basics.
learning_outcomes: Define an object, constructor and destructor to solve a problem
Define overloaded functions and operators
Use inheritance with the objects
Analyze the exceptions that appear in run time.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Java – NetBeans Environments – Variables - Selections
Hafta 4: One Dimensional Array & Multidimensional Arrays
resources: Y. Daniel Liang. Introduction to Java Programming, 10E, 2014.
Liang, Y. Daniel. Introduction to Java Programming Comprehensive Version. 8th Edition.

courses.name: PROBABILITY AND STATISTICS
courses.purpose: Ability to learn probability and statistics concepts and apply in practical problems.
courses.description: This course introduces probability and statistics for computer science students, including combinatorics, axioms of probability, conditional probability, discrete and continuous probability distributions, expectation and moments, hypothesis testing, parameter estimation, correlation and linear regression, and applications to computer science.
learning_outcomes: Understanding probability and statistics components
Able to compare probability distributions
Able to estimate statistical parameters
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to statistics, axioms of probability, sample spaces and events.
resources: Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Engineers and Computer Scientists, by S.M. Ross, Academic Press, 3rd or 4th Ed.

courses.name: SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS ENGINEERING
courses.purpose: Goal of this course is to introduce concepts of establishing, defining and managing the requirements for software systems.
courses.description: The course will involve building models of both requirements engineering process and requirements engineering product, concerning both functional and non-functional goals/requirements/specifications, using a systematic decision-making process.
learning_outcomes: Understand the importance of following systematic requirements engineering processes and techniques.
Effectively gather and analyze software requirements for the development of cost-effective and efficient technical solutions.
Use standard requirements engineering techniques for eliciting, analyzing, validating and managing software requirements.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Opening, introduction and giving general information about the course
Hafta 2: Explaining terms and definitons. Giving the definition of Requirement Engineering and sub branch.
Hafta 3: Discussing requirements elicitation techniques Functional and Nonfunctional requirements types
Hafta 4: Discussing requirements elicitation techniques Brainstorming Document Analysis Focus Group Interface Analysis Interviews Observation Prototype Requirement Workshops Surveys
Hafta 5: Discussing requirements analysis techniques Thinking Dialoguing Modelling
Hafta 6: Discussing requirements analysis techniques Diagramming Organizing
Hafta 8: Requirement Validation Templates and Checklists Peer Reviews Prototype
Hafta 9: Requirement Verification Inspection Analysis Demonstration Test Expert review
Hafta 10: Requirement Management Requirement Change Lifecycle Requirement Management Tools Test and Quality Standarts
resources: ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Requirements engineering standart document
Software Requirements, 3rd Edition, By Karl Wiegers, Joy Beatty

courses.name: SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
courses.purpose: This course aims at introducing basic concepts, applications, and processes of software project management.
courses.description: The course focuses on key characteristics of project management involving project scope management, project integration, project time management, quality and cost management, human resource and communications, procurement and risk management.
learning_outcomes: Proje yönetimi alanında teknik bilgi ve sosyal becerilerin kazandırılması.
Proje hazırlama ve proje yönetimi sürecinin aşamaları konularında teorik ve pratik bilgi birikiminin sağlanması.
Projelerin değerlendirilmesi konusunda teorik ve pratik bilgi birikiminin sağlanması.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Proje yönetim döngüsü, mantıksal çerçeve
Hafta 3: Proje yaşam döngüsü ve organizasyon
resources: Project Management Institute, A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (2008).

courses.name: BASIC PHOTOGRAPHY
courses.purpose: Providing basics for the art of photography.
courses.description: A course that focuses on the fundamentals of photography. The basic technical and artistic aspects of a photograph are covered. Methods for adjusting the light amount and principles of a successful composition are introduced to the participants. Ways of to understand photographs are discussed. The course content includes but is not limited to: Aperture, shutter speed, ISO, exposure, framing, rules of composition, modern photography equipment.
learning_outcomes: basic technical information on photography
basic aestethics information on photography
framing and composition knowledge
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Briefing on topic of the course and course methodology
resources: Understanding Exposure: How to Shoot Great Photographs with Any Camera — Bryan Peterson

courses.name: ENGINEERING ETHICS
courses.purpose: To socialize with the outstanding academic research on ethics from Business Ethics to Bioethics through a class-based interactive study of the influential papers on the interdisciplinary aspects of ethics from Business Ethics to Bioethics as well as with the implications of interdisciplinary ethics research for the Computer Engineers and for the society at large.
courses.description: The interdisciplinary aspects of outstanding ethics research from Business Ethics to Bioethics as well as the implications of ethics research for the Computer Engineers and for the society at large.
learning_outcomes: Reading the assigned academic paper in one of the interdisciplinary aspects of outstanding ethics research from Business Ethics to Bioethics in the light of students’ academic strengths and interests.
Taking notes from the paper
Carrying out additional research on the topical area of the paper
Presenting the progress periodically in the class
Contributing to the discussion of other papers in the class on the interdisciplinary aspects of outstanding ethics research from Business Ethics to Bioethics.
Preparing a short written report on the assigned paper using a template prepared by the Lecturer
Discussing the key findings of the paper in the light of the relevant academic research
Discussing the implications of the key findings of the paper for the Computer Engineers and the society at large
Making an oral presentation on the paper using a template prepared by the Lecturer.
Socializing with the other outstanding interdisciplinary research areas on Ethics from Business Ethics to Bioethics

Taking a written examination on the interdisciplinary aspects of Ethics from Business Ethics to Bioethics to reflect on the interdisciplinary aspects of the outstanding ethics research and their implications for Computer Engineers and for the society at large. The additional information would be provided at the course web site in www.okonur.info
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course introduction and overview Morals and ethics Comparison of ethics and engineering ethics Ethics at personal and student level
Hafta 2: The concept of professions The importance of ethics in science and engineering The role of codes of ethics Professional responsibilities of engineers
Hafta 3: The concept of morality The importance of core values Moral/ethical dilemmas and hierarchy of moral values Factors affecting moral responsibility, and degrees of responsibility
Hafta 4: Overview of ethical theories and applications
Hafta 5: Basics of ethical analyses and decision-making
Hafta 7: The importance if intention Truth (personal and social) The concept of whistleblowing
Hafta 8: Ethical leadership in engineering and society Conflicts of interests
Hafta 9: Engineers in organizations Ethics in the workplace Fairness (personal and social)
Hafta 10: Reliability, risk and safety Risk management Resource allocations
Hafta 11: Ethics in the electronic and digital age Privacy and confidentiality issue
Hafta 12: Responsible conduct of research Intellectual property and society
Hafta 13: Ethics and the environment Innovation and ethics
Hafta 14: Sustainable engineering Global and cultural considerations
resources: Engineering ethics Charles, Charles Byrns Fleddermann
Engineering Ethics, Charles B. Fleddermann

courses.name: ERGONOMICS
courses.purpose: This course is intended to provide an overview of the interdisciplinary field of ergonomics. Attention is devoted to provide the students with an in-depth understanding of the underlying principles of this discipline, ergonomics considerations in design/redesign, and research basis of ergonomics.
courses.description: The significant role of human in man-machine system. The general principles of physical and information ergonomics. The importance of anthropometric data and ergonomic principles in designing a workstation. The relation between ergonomics and efficiency. Psychological and injury effects of work. Examining the physical and social environment of work.
learning_outcomes: Grasping interdisciplinary interaction related to one’s area and developing and intensifying the current and high knowledge in that area based upon the competency in graduate level (knowledge).

By means of ability to use theoretical and practical information related to one’s area, to combine and interpret them with information from different disciplines producing new information and solving the faced problems by related searching methods (skill).

. By means of the ability to critically analyze knowledge, skills and also a study related to one’s area that requires expertise on that area, directing and continuing independently, developing new strategies for the problems that are not foreseen and taking the responsibilities together with fulfilling the leader role, the ability to produce solutions for that problems (competence to work independently, competence to take responsibility, competence to learning)

By means of the ability to promote current development and studies by supporting with qualitative and quantitative data and to use computer software together with information and communication technologies with a required level, critical analyzing, developing and altering, if required, social relationships and the norms directing these relationships, establishing written oral and visual communication with groups within one’s or different fields (communication and social competency).

Proficiency in a foreign language – at least European Language Portfolio B2 Level - and establishing written, oral and visual communication with that language for presenting one’s studies in the international environment (communication and social competency).

By means of the ability to inspect the steps like gathering, interpreting, implementing and announcing related data with the one’s area by overseeing scientific, cultural and ethical norms, teaching these norms, developing strategy, policy and action plans in related subjects and evaluating the obtained results by making the use of quality processes, using the gathered information and solving problems and/or implementation skills in the interdisciplinary strategies (area specific competency).
resources: Kroemer, K.H.E., Kroemer, H.B., Kroemer-Elbert, K.E., Ergonomics How to Design for Ease and Efficiency, Prentice Hall International.
Pulat, B.M., (1992), Fundamentals of IE, Waveland Press

courses.name: SCIENTIFIC COMMUNICATION
courses.purpose: Provide an understanding of the historical conventions of science communication and their impact on the organization, use and distribution of scientific knowledge. Provide factual knowledge about the publication process, including preparation of a manuscript, interaction with journal submission systems and the review process. Provide skills for communicating specialist knowledge to science peers and to non-specialist audiences. Provide an understanding of copyrights and intentional/unintentional plagiarism.

Analyze and critically evaluate the role of science in local, national and global community.
Find and use resources for preparing science articles, proposals or public presentations.
Give, receive and value criticism in the form of peer review.

Hafta 8: Structure and components of a standard journal article.

Hafta 10: Visual presentation and figure preparation

He/she knows the induction, recursion and proof techniques and can apply these techniques for the problems in computer science.
He/she knows how to work with the abstract mathematical structures that are used to represent discrete objects and relationship between these objects which are also called discrete structures.

courses.name: CONCEPTS OF PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
courses.purpose: This course explains the main concepts of programming languages.
courses.description: Language evaluation criteria. Describing syntax and semantics. Tools for constructing lexical and syntactical analyzers. Names, bindings, type checking, and scopes. Data types. Expressions and the assignment statement. Statement-level control structures. Subprograms. Abstract data types. Concurrency. Exception handling. Functional programming languages. Logic programming.
learning_outcomes: It will give you a framework for understanding how to use language constructs effectively and how to design correct and elegant programs.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Introduction, Preliminaries, and History of programming languages
Hafta 6: Names, Bindings, Type checking, and Scoping
resources: Robert W.Sebesta, Concepts of Programming Languages, Global Edition, 11/E, Pearson.
Watt, David A., Programming Language Design Concepts Wiley , 2004

courses.name: SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
courses.purpose: Building on previous exposure to the fundamentals of the software process, this course focuses on techniques for ensuring software quality. Here, quality assurance is viewed as an activity that runs through the entire development process: understanding the needs of clients and users; analyzing and documenting requirements; verifying and validating solutions through testing.
courses.description: Requirements elicitation and user-centered development. Requirements analysis. Verification and validation. Quality management.
learning_outcomes: Students who pass the course satisfactorily will be able to design and implement quality improvement aspects of industrial plants
Students who pass the course satisfactorily will be able to describe the management skills involved in quality assurance
Students who pass the course satisfactorily will be able use, plan, and organise quality techniques in the measurement of quality
resources: D.C. Montgomery, Introduction to Statistical Quality Control, John Wiley and Sons Co., 6th Edition, (2009)
Oakland, J. “Total Quality Management Text with Cases”, Butterworth-Heinemann, 3 Edition, 2003.
P. Ammann and J. Offutt. Introduction to Software Testing. Cambridge University Press, 2008. ISBN 978-0-521-88038-1
Summers, D. “Quality Management”, Prentice Hall, 5th Edition, 2009.

courses.name: DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce concepts and principles of database management systems and teach how to apply them in modelling and solving real-life problems and applications.
courses.description: Lectures will include a review of relational databases, relational data model, database design, SQL query language, query processing and performance tuning, and transaction processing. Relational databases and data model; database design (ER, EER); SQL query language (DDL, DML); query processing and performance tuning; transaction processing and concurrency; indexes and physical design.

Understanding ER and EER diagrams and able to making designs using these diagrams
Understanding relational algebra and its relation with SQL
Able to do query and update operations using SQL
Able to normalize a database
Understanding the basics of performance tuning
Understanding the concurrency control techniques

courses.name: DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
courses.purpose: This course teaches widely-used algorithms and their complexities. It aims to help students develop and analyze algorithms for common problems.
courses.description: Introduction to algorithms; algorithm design and problem solving strategies; complexity analysis; stable matching; GCD; Big-oh analysis and recurrence relations; sorting algorithms; searching algorithms; graphs; dynamic programming; NP-completeness.
learning_outcomes: analyze the time and space complexity of algorithms

efficiently solve problems with Greedy algorithms that can be modeled directly or through a sequence of transformations as instances of interval scheduling, interval partitioning, scheduling to minimize lateness, clustering, and minimum-cost arborescence problems
design and implement efficient divide and conquer algorithms suitable to solve a particular computational problem
model complex problems in terms of graphs
search for an efficient solution that is made possible by the applications of the graph algorithms
have awareness of the lower bound associated with sorting and the conditions in which it holds.

Hafta 3: Time and space complexity analysis
Hafta 10: Minimum spanning trees (kruskal algorithm, prim algorithm), shortest path (bellman-ford algorithm, dijkstra algorithm)

courses.name: OPERATING SYSTEMS
courses.purpose: The goals of this course are to teach students the fundamental tasks of a general-purpose operating system and the main approach and algorithms which the operating system employs in order to fulfill these tasks; to allow students to get familiar with managing computer hardware and by this way to equip them with basic information which allows them to develop system programs close to computer hardware.
courses.description: Introduction to computer operating systems; processes, threads, interprocess communication, process scheduling, process synchronization, deadlocks, memory management and virtual memory, file systems - interface and implementation, mass-storage structure and management, input/output systems, examples from operating systems such as Linux and Windows.
learning_outcomes: The student remembers previously learned basic knowledge about computer hardware and underlines the relationship between the hardware and the operating system.

The student understands how an operating system handles process management, memory management, input/output management and file management tasks; he/she also comprehends the working structure of the multithreading model.

The student notices the similarity of some administrative problems of an operating system to some real life problems; and to solve these problems he/she determines the appropiate algorithms or develops new ones.
The student implements the algorithms encountered in the operating systems using a modern programming language.

The student understands and gains detailed knowledge about the working principles of modern operating systems; using this knowledge he/she effectively manages computer hardware in his/her own developed system programs.
The student acquires introductory knowledge about parallel programming.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Overview of an Operating System, Historical Evolution of Operating Systems
Hafta 5: Critical Region and Solutions to Critical Region Problem : Software and Hardware-based Solutions, Semaphores
Hafta 9: Memory Management : Paging and Segmentation
Hafta 13: Hard Disc Management, Input/Output Management
resources: Abraham Silberschatz, Peter Galvin, Greg Gagne, Applied Operating System Concepts, 1st ed., John Wiley & Sons, Inc, 2000.

courses.name: SOFTWARE DESIGN PATTERNS
courses.purpose: At the end of this course, the student will understand the motivation for object-oriented design patterns, their terminology and the GoF design patterns. Students will be able to apply design patterns in suitable contexts and justify this application. In addition, students will be able to identify design issues and propose suitable solutions.
courses.description: The content of the course is on studying the GoF software design patterns. Implementation of the design patterns is conducted through a set of programming homework assignments and a project. The students will continually apply UML class and sequence diagrams throughout the course.
learning_outcomes: Differentiate an existing architecture or design patterns. Generate and justify architecture and/or design given a collection of requirements. Produce and present concise and unambiguous architecture and design descriptions. Create and implement an architecture and design, refining it into a complete system.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Overview of UML class and sequence diagrams. Introduction to the objectives of software design. Introduction to software design patterns: objectives, benefits and classification. Code smells and anti-patterns.
Hafta 2: Creational design patterns: Abstract Factory and Factory Method
Hafta 3: Creational design patterns: Builder, Prototype and Singleton patterns.
Hafta 4: Tutorial: Design scenarios for the creational patterns
Hafta 5: Structural Patterns: Adapter (Class), Adapter (Object) and Proxy patterns.
Hafta 6: Structural Patterns: Bridge and Composite patterns.
Hafta 7: Structural Patterns: Decorator and Facade patterns.
Hafta 9: Behavioral Patterns: Template Method and Chain of Responsibility patterns.
Hafta 10: Tutorial: Design scenarios for the structural patterns.
Hafta 11: Behavioral Patterns: Command and Iterator patterns.
Hafta 12: Behavioral Patterns: Mediator, Memento and Observer patterns.
Hafta 13: Behavioral Patterns: State, Strategy and Visitor patterns.
Hafta 14: Tutorial: Design scenarios for the behavioral patterns.
resources: Refactoring to Patterns. Joshua Kerievsky. Aug 2004. Addison Wesley.
Design Patterns For Dummies Steve Holzner. 2006. Wiley.
Gamma, Helm, Johnson, and Vlissides. Design Patterns : Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software. Addison-Wesley, 1994

learning_outcomes: be able to: describe the theory and practice of compilation, in particular, the lexical analysis, parsing and code generation and optimization phases of compilation, and design a compiler for a concise programming language.

courses.name: HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION

The course aims to equip students with skills in user analysis, interaction and interface design, prototyping, and effective integration of HCI practices into software development processes.

Analyze user needs and define UX requirements.
Apply interaction and interface design principles to software systems.
Develop low- and high-fidelity prototypes.
Conduct usability and accessibility evaluations.
Integrate HCI practices into software development processes.

courses.name: FORMAL LANGUAGES WITH ABSTRACT MACHINES
courses.purpose: Detailed study of abstract models for the syntax of programming languages and information processing devices. Languages and their representation; grammars; finite automata and regular sets; context-free grammars and pushdown automata; Chomsky Hierarchy; closure properties of families of languages; syntax analysis.
courses.description: The course covers formal language concepts and abstract machine models, including languages and their representation, grammars, finite automata, regular sets, context-free grammars, pushdown automata, the Chomsky hierarchy, closure properties of language families, and syntax analysis.
learning_outcomes: Determine the applications of fundamental models of computer theory in computer science
Identify the theoretical base of sequential circuits in digital logic design concepts
Reflect abstract thinking
State concepts and analysis formally
Prove why or why not a language is in a given complexity class using pumping lemmas, closure properties, reductions, and other mathematically rigorous mechanisms
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Revise languages. Syntax of the lambda calculus. Parsing arithmetic expressions. Introduction to PLAI language.
Hafta 2: Abstract and concrete syntax for arithmetic and lambda calculus Introducing identifiers to our arithmetic
Hafta 3: Semantics for the lambda calculus - alpha beta eta reductions Problems with identifier substitution.
Hafta 4: Substitution for the lambda calculus - a consistent rule. Substitution for arithmetic expressions with identifiers. Static (lexical) scope. Dynamic scope is a bug.
Hafta 5: Building program constructs in the lambda calculus. Eliminating identifiers. De Bruijn notation.
Hafta 6: Church numerals and beginning arithmetic in the lambda calculus. Deferring substitution - environments.
Hafta 8: Data structures in the lambda calculus. Introducing functions (not first class). Moving on to first class functions.
Hafta 9: Normal form. Sentences without normal form. The Church-Rosser property. First class functions and closures.
Hafta 10: Recursion in the lambda calculus. The Y operator. Recursion with first class functions. Cyclic environments
Hafta 11: Higher order functions in the lambda calculus. Metacircular and syntactic interpretation.
Hafta 12: Proving the lambda calculus is Church Rosser. Introducing state.
Hafta 13: Bigger lambda calculus programs. Exploring state - the store.
resources: Daniel P. Friedman and Mitchell Wand, Essentials of Programming Languages, 3rd Edition MIT Press 2008.
Introduction to the Theory of Computation, 3rd Edition Michael Sipser ISBN: 978-1-133-18779-0

Demonstrate an understanding of memory management, the protected mode and the virtual 8086 mode employed in the lab
Demonstrate an understanding of how hardware and software interrupts work

courses.name: NUMERICAL ANALYSIS
courses.purpose: This course is an introduction to numerical analysis. The primary objective is to develop numerical solution algorithms to solve mathematical problems which generally do not have analytical solutions. This course helps students to understand mathematical theory of algorithms, detecting and controlling errors in scientific computing.
courses.description: Error analysis in numerical computations; computer number systems; floating point arithmetic conversion from base 10 to base 2; floating point systems and round-off errors. Linear system of equations: direct methods, Gauss elimination with backward substitution, pivoting strategies, LU factorization and forward substitution, Crout factorization. Numerical factorizations: Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel methods and their convergence analysis. Eigenvalue problems, power method, orthogonal factorization, least square problems. Solutions of nonlinear equations in one variable: locating roots of equations, fixed point iteration technique, bisection method, Newton-Raphson method, Regula-Falsi and Secant methods.
learning_outcomes: Able to predict, detect, understand and control errors when approximating solutions to problems using numerical algorithms with computers
Able to understand convergence, accuracy and stability of numerical algorithms
Able to obtain solution algorithms numerically for linear system of equations
Able to solve nonlinear equations numerically
Able to apply the basic iterative methods(fixed point, bisection, Regula-Falsi,Newton-Raphson etc. ) for the solution of nonlinear equations in one variable
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Error analysis in numerical computations -Computer number systems -Floating point arithmetic conversion from base 10 to base 2 -Floating point systems and round-off errors
Hafta 2: Linear system of equations: Direct methods; -Gauss elimination with backward substitution -Pivoting strategies
resources: Richard L. Burden and J. Douglas Faires, Numerical Analysis, 2011, 9th Edition, (available in AYBU library on the shelve QA297 B84 2011)
E. Ward Cheney, David R. Kincaid, Numerical Mathematics and Computing,1999, Brooks/Cole Publishing Company, 4th Edition.

courses.name: ADVANCED DATABASES
courses.purpose: The objective of this course is to equip students—beyond traditional relational database management systems—with advanced data modeling, query optimization, consistency and concurrency control, and performance-tuning techniques in the context of big data, distributed architectures, and NoSQL/NewSQL approaches; it also aims to develop their ability to design and apply modern database technologies in real-world applications.
courses.description: The course covers the architecture of relational database engines, query optimization and indexing techniques, concurrency control and consistency protocols, distributed database systems, and NoSQL/NewSQL approaches; it also includes hands-on exercises with big data technologies such as Hadoop and Spark SQL, OLAP and data warehousing solutions, real-time stream processing, data security and privacy, cloud-based database services, and current industry trends.
learning_outcomes: Gain practical experience in database systems
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: The course covers the architecture of relational database engines, SQL query optimization and execution plans, indexing techniques such as B-tree, hash, and bitmap; ACID principles, lock mechanisms, and concurrency control with data consistency over MVCC; distributed database systems with Two-Phase Commit, Paxos, and Raft protocols; NoSQL and NewSQL approaches with document, key-value, column-store, and graph models; big data processing over Hadoop ecosystem and Spark SQL; data warehouse solutions with star/snowflake schemas and OLAP cubes; real-time streaming data processing using Kafka, Flink, and Spark Streaming; data security, privacy, and authorization methods; cloud-based database services with DBaaS models; and current trends such as multi-model databases, serverless architectures, and AI-assisted optimization.
resources: Database Management Systems — Raghu Ramakrishnan & Johannes Gehrke
Database System Concepts — Abraham Silberschatz, Henry F. Korth, S. Sudarshan

courses.name: SYSTEM ADMINISTRATION
courses.purpose: By the end of the course, the students should have a good understanding of the functions of a Unix/Linux file system and shell-level interactions with it. They should also understand how shell-level programs can perform useful tasks, gain experience with shell scripts, gain experience with the C programming language, and become proficient using pointers, arrays, strings, and storage allocation. In addition, they should be familiar with Unix system calls for process creation and interaction.
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to programming in the Unix environment using the C language. It covers the Unix kernel, Unix shell, file system and basic shell commands, Unix utilities and shell programming, the C programming language (functions, arrays, strings, pointers, dynamic memory allocation, low-level operators and bit fields), and Unix system calls including process control, signal handling, and inter-process communication.
learning_outcomes: This course provides an introduction to programming in the Unix environment using the C language.

courses.name: EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce embedded systems and the high-level design approaches needed to handle their increasing complexity. It emphasizes hardware/software tradeoffs, unified design methods, and modern tools rather than low-level assembly programming and logic design.
courses.description: The course covers the fundamentals of embedded systems, including trends and challenges, the design and use of single-purpose and general-purpose processors, memories and buses, hardware/software tradeoffs, digital camera examples, advanced computation models, control systems, chip technologies, and modern design tools.
learning_outcomes: In today's world, embedded systems are everywhere -- homes, offices, cars, factories, hospitals, plans and consumer electronics. Their huge numbers and new complexity call for a new design approach, one that emphasizes high-level tools and hardware/software tradeoffs, rather than low-level assembly-language programming and logic design. This book presents the traditionally distinct fields of software and hardware design in a new unified approach. It covers trends and challenges, introduces the design and use of single-purpose processors ("hardware") and general-purpose processors ("software"), describes memories and buses, illustrates hardware/software tradeoffs using a digital camera example, and discusses advanced computation models, control systems, chip technologies, and modern design tools. Below is the table of contents of ESD. Furthermore, ESD intentionally does not cover the details of any particular processor, in large part because of the variety of setups used in embedded systems courses. Below, however, are extensive resources to assist teachers with the laboratory part of a course based on ESD. Presentation slides for each chapter can also be found, along with additional chapter resources.
resources: Gajski, Daniel D. Principles of Digital Design, New Jersey: Prentice-Hall, 1997. ISBN 0-13-301144-5. Describes combinational and sequential logic design, with a focus on optimization techniques, CAD, and higher-levels of design.

Hafta 2: Unconstrained optimization, definition of local minima, convexity and global minima, necessary and sufficient conditions, linear regression example
Hafta 3: Line search methods, step length, the Wolfe conditions, backtracking, bisection method, secant method, convergence rate, steepest descent
Hafta 4: Newton’s method and variants, trust-region methods
Hafta 5: Linear conjugate gradient method, conjugate directions
Hafta 6: Rate of convergence, preconditioning, common preconditioners
Hafta 7: The Fletcher–Reeves Method and The Polak–Ribiere Method as nonlinear conjugate gradient methods
Hafta 8: Quasi-Newton methods, definition of Jacobian and Hessian, comparison to Newton method, common algorithms: Davidon–Fletcher–Powell (DFP) formula
Hafta 9: Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (BFGS) algorithm, Broyden's method, and symmetric rank-one (SR1)
Hafta 10: Linear least-squares problems, pseudo-inverse, and linearization
Hafta 11: The Gauss-Newton and the Levenberg-Marquardt methods for non-linear least-squares problems
Hafta 12: Constrained optimization, equality and inequality constraint, first-order optimality conditions, a fundamental necessary condition, second-order (sufficient) conditions
Hafta 14: Duality, convex optimization, penalty and barrier methods

courses.name: NEURAL NETWORKS
courses.purpose: This course will introduce the fundamental principles and algorithms of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) systems.
courses.description: The course covers the main neural network architectures and learning algorithms, including perceptrons and the LMS algorithm, back propagation learning, radial basis function networks, support vector machines, Kohonen’s self organizing feature maps, Hopfield networks, and artificial neural networks for signal processing, pattern recognition, and control.
learning_outcomes: Adequate knowledge in Mathematics, Science and Software Engineering ability to use theoretical and applied information in these areas to model and solve Software Engineering problems
Ability to identify, define, formulate, and solve complex Software Engineering problems ability to select and apply proper analysis and modeling methods for this purpose

Ability to design, implement, verify, validate, measure and maintain a complex software system, process or product under realistic constraints and conditions, in such a way as to meet the desired result ability to apply modern methods for this purpose
Ability to devise, select, and use modern techniques and tools needed for Software Engineering practice
Ability to design and conduct experiments, gather data, analyze and interpret results for investigating Software Engineering problems
Ability to work efficiently in Software Engineering disciplinary and multi-disciplinary teams ability to work individually
Ability to communicate effectively in Turkish, both orally and in writing knowledge of a minimum of two foreign languages
Recognition of the need for lifelong learning ability to access information, to follow developments in science and technology, and to continue to educate him/herself
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Biological motivation. Historical remarks on artificial neural networks. Applications of artificial neural networks. A taxonomy of artificial neural network models and learning algorithms.

Hafta 2: General artificial neuron model. Discretevalued perceptron model, threshold logic and their limitations. Discretetime (dynamical) Hopfield networks. Hebb’s rule. Connection wieght matrix as an outer product of memory patterns.

Hafta 3: Supervised learning. Perceptron learning algorithm. Adaptive linear element. Supervised learning as output error minimization problem. Gradient descent algorithm for minimization. Least mean square rule.

Hafta 4: Single layer, continuous valued perceptron. Nonlinear (sigmoidal) activation function. Delta rule. Batch mode and pattern mode gradient descent algorithms. Convergence conditions for deterministic and stochastic gradient descent algorithms.
Hafta 5: Multi layer perceptron as universal approximator. Function representation and approximation problems. Backpropagation Learning. Local minima problem. Overtraining.

Hafta 6: Midterm Exam I. Batch and pattern mode training. Training set versus test set. Overfitting problem. General practices for network training and testing. Signal processing and pattern recognition applications of multilayer perceptrons.

Hafta 7: Radial Basis Function (RBF) network. Backpropagation learning for determining linear weights, centers and widths parameters of RBF networks. Random selection of centers. Input versus inputoutput clustering for center and width determination. Regularization theory, mixture of Gaussion (conditional probability density function) model and neurofuzzy connections of RBF networks.

Hafta 8: Support vector machines for classification. Kernel representations. Generalization ability. VapnikChervonenkis dimension. Support vector regression. Comparison of different kernels, loss (error) functions and norms for (separating hyerplane) flatness.

Hafta 9: Parametric versus nonparametric methods for data representation. Unsupervised learning as a vector quantization problem. Competetive networks. Winner take all network. Kohonen’s selforganizing feature map. Clustering.

Hafta 10: Continuous time Hopfield networks. Stability analysis of multiple equilibria of Hopfield networks. Hopfield networks for cost minimization: Lyapunov (energy) based design of Hopfield networks. Associative memory. Traveling salesman problem. Combinatorial optimization.

Hafta 11: Midterm Exam II. Signal processing applications of artificial neural networks. Principle component analysis. Data compression and reduction. Image and 1D signal compression and transformation applications of artificial neural networks.

Hafta 12: Pattern recognition applications of artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks for feature extraction. Nonlinear feature mapping. Data fusion. Artificial neural networks as classifiers. Image and speech recognition applications.

Hafta 13: Control applications of artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks for system identification. Artificial neural networks as controllers. Inverse systems design. Direct and indirect control methods. Adaptive control applications.
Hafta 14: Implementation of artificial neural networks models and associated learning algorithms for signal processing, pattern recognition and control in MATLAB numerical software environment.
resources: Lecture Notes
S. Haykin, Neural Networks and Learning Machines, Pearson Education, 3rd Ed., 2009
Dive into Deep Learning (https://d2l.ai/)

courses.name: GREEN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to introduce students to the concepts and principles of Green Software Engineering and to equip them with sustainability-oriented approaches in software development processes. Throughout the course, students will learn how to analyze the environmental impact of software systems and how to design, develop, and evaluate energy-efficient and sustainable software solutions. The course also aims to develop students’ decision-making skills with a sustainability perspective in areas such as cloud computing, data management, artificial intelligence, user experience design, and the software development lifecycle.
courses.description: This course examines Green Software Engineering from both theoretical and practical perspectives. The course covers the relationship between sustainability and software, focusing on the impact of software systems on energy consumption and carbon footprint. Topics include energy-efficient software development techniques, sustainable software architectures, the environmental impact of cloud computing, sustainable data management approaches, and green data centers. The course also addresses the environmental impact of artificial intelligence and machine learning, the green software development lifecycle, sustainable user experience design, energy efficiency in mobile and embedded systems, the role of open-source software in sustainability, and methods for measuring and reporting software sustainability. The course concludes with discussions on future trends in green software engineering and final project presentations.
learning_outcomes: Explain the concepts and principles of Green Software Engineering and the relationship between sustainability and software.
Analyze the energy consumption and environmental impact of software systems.
Apply energy-efficient and sustainable software development techniques.
Compare sustainable software architectures and evaluate appropriate architectural approaches.
Assess the environmental impact of cloud computing, data management, and data centers.
Analyze the environmental impact of artificial intelligence and machine learning applications and propose green approaches.
Integrate the Green Software Development Lifecycle (Green SDLC) into software projects.
Apply sustainable user experience (UX) design principles to software interfaces.
Develop solutions to improve energy efficiency in mobile and embedded systems.
Measure and report sustainability metrics in software projects.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Green Software Engineering
Hafta 2: Energy efficiency in software development
Hafta 6: Environmental Impact of AI and Machine Learning
Hafta 9: Mobile and Embedded Systems Sustainability
Hafta 10: Legal and Ethical Considerations in Green Software
Hafta 11: Open Source and Green Software
Hafta 12: Measuring and Reporting Software Sustainability
Hafta 13: Future Trends in Green Software Engineering
resources: Green Software Engineering: Exploring Green Technology for Sustainable IT Solutions, 1st Edition Author: Santiago Fontanarrosa Publisher: Packt Publishing Pvt. Ltd. Year: 2024 ISBN: 9781835885888; 1835885888
